abstract:
Extracellular enzymes in soil and sedimentary environments are critical drivers of nutrient cycling, and their functional longevity is traditionally thought to be enhanced via adsorption onto mineral surfaces. While the oxygenation of mineral-bound Fe(II) is known to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS), the impact of this process on the activity and lifespan of these enzymes has remained elusive. In this study, we investigated how the oxidation of mineral-bound Fe(II) affects the hydrolytic performance of $\beta$-glucosidase (BG), a key cellulose-degrading enzyme. Using three model minerals—pre-reduced nontronite and montmorillonite (clay minerals) and magnetite (an iron oxide)—at pH 5 and 7, we found that redox conditions fundamentally redefine the enzyme-mineral relationship.

Under anoxic conditions, adsorption to mineral surfaces suppressed immediate BG activity but successfully shielded the enzyme, extending its functional life. However, upon exposure to oxygen, the minerals generated ROS, with hydroxyl radical ($\bullet$OH) production correlating directly with the extent of structural Fe(II) oxidation. These ROS—specifically $\bullet$OH—triggered conformational unfolding and structural decomposition of the BG molecule, leading to a rapid loss of activity and a significantly shortened lifespan. Our findings reveal that in oxic environments, the damaging effects of ROS override the protective benefits typically offered by mineral adsorption. This study identifies a previously unrecognized mechanism of enzyme inactivation and suggests that predicting the active enzyme pool in redox-dynamic environments requires accounting for the potent inhibitory role of Fe(II)-bearing minerals.
